name: Glommio
summary: Rust thread-per-core async runtime by Datadog where each worker thread runs an independent single-threaded event loop bound to a CPU core via affinity, with its own io_uring instance; rejects work-stealing in exchange for perfect cache locality and zero cross-core contention.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Glommio** (Datadog, originally for ScyllaDB-adjacent workloads) is the canonical Rust thread-per-core io_uring runtime. Each worker thread runs its own independent single-threaded event loop bound to a specific CPU core via affinity, with its own `io_uring` instance for asynchronous I/O. Inter-core coordination is by explicit message passing through bounded channels rather than work-stealing across a shared scheduler.

Futures are pinned to one core for their lifetime, eliminating cross-core synchronisation on the hot path. The trade-off is sharp: perfect cache locality and zero shared-state contention, at the cost of needing to manually balance work across cores when the workload isn't naturally per-connection or per-shard.

sections.title: Contrast with Tokio's shared-scheduler model
sections.content:
Distinct from Tokio's M:N work-stealing model where futures freely migrate between worker threads: Glommio futures are pinned to one core for their lifetime. Cross-core synchronisation cost is zero on the data path, paid only on explicit channel sends.

Distinct from ActiveJ Eventloop + Workers (`data/activej-eventloop`): ActiveJ's primary/worker topology balances *connections* across worker eventloops at accept time and lets each worker run arbitrary code; Glommio expects the application to organise itself around per-core ownership of long-lived state.

sections.title: Production use
sections.content:
Production use: ScyllaDB-class databases where every connection is a long-lived shard owner, Cloudflare's pingora proxy variants, Datadog internal services. The pattern fits workloads where every connection or shard is a long-lived independent unit of work — the per-core ownership model maps cleanly to per-connection state.

Status (as of 2026-04): stable, MIT-licensed, maintained by Datadog. Less mainstream than Tokio but the canonical reference for thread-per-core Rust async.
